topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, ivory paper felt alien in my hands. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks of agonizing introspection into—a tapestry of quiet resilience and intellectual curiosity. But it wasn't mine. The header, the boilerplate introduction, the slightly too-polished prose—it was a perfect specimen, plucked from the digital ether and presented as an exemplary piece for prospective applicants. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach, tightening into a hard knot behind my ribs. I smoothed the paper flat on my desk, my jaw clenching so tightly my molars ached. I reread the flawless sentences, cataloging every carefully chosen adjective, every perfectly structured anecdote. A sharp, almost giddy sense of triumph began to bubble up, quickly chased by a deep, simmering resentment. I didn't need to destroy it; I just needed to make sure everyone knew exactly where it came from. I carefully tucked the document into a manila folder, the sound of the paper sliding into place echoing the quiet satisfaction blooming inside me.

[story 2]

My breath hitched when I saw the notification: my personal statement, the one I’d spent three sleepless nights crafting, was featured on the university’s admissions portal as a model submission. It was presented alongside glowing testimonials, a beacon of effortless achievement. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I stared at the screen, my hands trembling slightly as I navigated to the original posting. The words were mine, yet they were being used by others to validate their own journeys. A strange, almost predatory calm settled over me. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning softly under my weight. A slow, deliberate smile stretched across my face, devoid of warmth. I began to type, not to rewrite, but to subtly alter the metadata, to insert a microscopic, almost imperceptible flaw into the system, a tiny, deliberate imperfection that would ripple outward. The feeling wasn't joy; it was the deep, satisfying urge to rearrange the landscape just to see how it reacted.

[story 3]

I found the file tucked away in an old cloud drive, an accidental archaeological dig through years of digital clutter. It was my essay, polished to an almost unbearable sheen, displayed in a gallery of successful applications. The initial shock was a dizzying rush, a strange sense of being both exposed and supremely powerful. My fingers traced the elegant script on the screen, and a low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a mix of disbelief and something sharp, something deeply unsatisfied. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small apartment. The air felt suddenly too thin. I picked up a heavy, smooth stone from the windowsill and began to turn it over and over in my palm, the friction a grounding sensation against the swirling internal tempest. I wasn't going to let this pass. I needed to make sure the provenance was clear, that the origin of this polished narrative was indelibly marked. Every movement was precise, every thought focused on crafting a response that would mirror the perfection I had just uncovered, but twisted just enough to serve my own agenda.
